expanded_description: Understand the fundamental properties and definitions of acids and bases. Learn to identify acids and bases by their characteristics, formulas, and behavior in aqueous solutions, laying the foundation for acid-base chemistry.

core_concept.principle: Acids and Bases Are Defined by Their Behavior in Water
core_concept.explanation: Acids and bases are two fundamental classes of compounds with opposing properties. Acids produce hydrogen ions ($\text{H}^+$) in aqueous solution, while bases produce hydroxide ions ($\text{OH}^-$) or accept hydrogen ions. These definitions, along with characteristic properties like taste, conductivity, and reactivity, enable identification and classification of acids and bases in chemical systems.

image.alt: Diagram comparing acid and base properties with examples
image.caption: Figure 9.6.1: Acids and bases exhibit opposing properties: acids taste sour and turn litmus red; bases taste bitter and turn litmus blue.

sections.section_title: Characteristic Properties of Acids and Bases
sections.introduction: Acids and bases can be identified by their distinctive physical and chemical properties. While tasting chemicals is never safe in the laboratory, these characteristic properties help chemists classify compounds and predict their behavior.

key_concept.definition: Acids are compounds that produce $\text{H}^+$ ions in aqueous solution, taste sour, turn blue litmus red, conduct electricity, and react with metals to produce hydrogen gas. Bases are compounds that produce $\text{OH}^-$ ions or accept $\text{H}^+$ in aqueous solution, taste bitter, feel slippery, turn red litmus blue, and conduct electricity.
key_concept.context: These properties arise from the ions produced in solution: $\text{H}^+$ gives acids their characteristic behavior; $\text{OH}^-$ gives bases theirs.

components.interpretation: Acids ionize to release $\text{H}^+$; bases dissociate to release $\text{OH}^-$ or accept $\text{H}^+$. These ions determine solution properties.

image.alt: Diagram showing acid and base ionization in water
image.caption: Figure 9.6.2: Acids release $\text{H}^+$ ions; bases release $\text{OH}^-$ ions in aqueous solution.

classifications.value: Sour Taste, Litmus Red, Reactive with Metals
characteristics: Taste sour (citric acid in lemons, acetic acid in vinegar)
Turn blue litmus paper red
Conduct electricity in aqueous solution (electrolytes)
React with active metals to produce $\text{H}_2$ gas
React with bases to form salt and water (neutralization)
classifications.behavior: Acid properties stem from $\text{H}^+$ ions: sour taste from $\text{H}^+$ stimulating taste receptors; conductivity from mobile ions; metal reaction from $\text{H}^+$ reduction.

examples.explanation: Strong acid; ionizes completely: $\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq)$. Reacts with Zn: $\text{Zn}(s) + 2\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{ZnCl}_2(aq) + \text{H}_2(g)$.

image.caption: Acids react with active metals to produce hydrogen gas.

examples.explanation: Weak organic acid; gives lemons and oranges their sour taste; safe to consume in food amounts.

image.caption: Natural acids provide sour taste in foods.

classifications.value: Bitter Taste, Slippery Feel, Litmus Blue
characteristics: Taste bitter (caffeine, baking soda)
Feel slippery or soapy (saponification of skin oils)
Turn red litmus paper blue
Conduct electricity in aqueous solution (electrolytes)
React with acids to form salt and water (neutralization)
classifications.behavior: Base properties stem from $\text{OH}^-$ ions: slippery feel from $\text{OH}^-$ reacting with skin oils; conductivity from mobile ions.

examples.explanation: Strong base; dissociates completely: $\text{NaOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq)$. Feels slippery; reacts with acids: $\text{NaOH} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$.

image.caption: Strong bases feel slippery and conduct electricity.

examples.explanation: Weak base; accepts $\text{H}^+$: $\text{NH}_3(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_4^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq)$. Common household cleaner.

image.caption: Weak bases like ammonia are common in household cleaners.

characteristics: Many acids and bases are corrosive and dangerous
Tasting is never permitted in laboratory settings
Use indicators (litmus, pH paper) to test acidity/basicity
Wear proper PPE: goggles, gloves, lab coat
classifications.behavior: While taste is a characteristic property, it is NEVER used for identification in the lab due to severe safety risks.

examples.explanation: Extremely corrosive; causes severe burns on contact. Never taste—use pH paper or indicators instead.

image.caption: Always use indicators, never taste, to identify acids and bases.

examples.explanation: Caustic base; causes severe chemical burns. Handle with gloves and eye protection; never taste.

image.caption: Proper PPE is essential when working with bases.

principles.statement: Litmus paper is a simple indicator: blue litmus turns red in acid; red litmus turns blue in base. No color change indicates neutral solution.
principles.application: To test an unknown solution: dip blue litmus—if it turns red, solution is acidic; if no change, dip red litmus—if it turns blue, solution is basic.
principles.analogy: Like a traffic light: red means acid, blue means base, no change means neutral—simple visual identification.

principles.statement: Both acids and bases conduct electricity in aqueous solution because they produce mobile ions ($\text{H}^+$, $\text{OH}^-$, and counterions).
principles.application: A solution that conducts electricity and turns litmus red is likely an acid; one that conducts and turns litmus blue is likely a base.
principles.example: This principle is used in conductivity meters to monitor acid-base reactions in industrial processes.

sections.section_title: Arrhenius Definition of Acids and Bases
sections.introduction: The Arrhenius definition, proposed by Svante Arrhenius in 1884, provides the first systematic framework for understanding acids and bases based on their behavior in aqueous solution.

key_concept.definition: Arrhenius acid: a substance that produces hydrogen ions ($\text{H}^+$) when dissolved in water. Arrhenius base: a substance that produces hydroxide ions ($\text{OH}^-$) when dissolved in water.
key_concept.context: This definition is limited to aqueous solutions but provides a clear foundation for understanding acid-base behavior and neutralization reactions.

categories.description: Arrhenius acids contain hydrogen and ionize in water to release $\text{H}^+$ ions. The $\text{H}^+$ ion is often written as $\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$ (hydronium ion) to reflect its actual form in water.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Acid ionization: $\text{HA}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow \text{H}_3\text{O}^+(aq) + \text{A}^-(aq)$. Strong acids ionize completely; weak acids ionize partially.

examples.explanation: Strong acid: $\text{HCl}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow \text{H}_3\text{O}^+(aq) + \text{Cl}^-(aq)$. Ionizes 100%; high conductivity.

image.caption: Strong acids ionize completely in water.

examples.explanation: Weak acid: $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_3\text{O}^+(aq) + \text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-(aq)$. Partial ionization; equilibrium favors reactants.

image.caption: Weak acids establish equilibrium with partial ionization.

examples.explanation: Strong diprotic acid: first $\text{H}^+$ ionizes completely; second $\text{H}^+$ ionizes partially. $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{H}^+ + \text{HSO}_4^-$; $\text{HSO}_4^- \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{SO}_4^{2-}$.

image.caption: Polyprotic acids release multiple $\text{H}^+$ ions.

categories.description: Arrhenius bases contain hydroxide ions or produce them in water. Metal hydroxides dissociate to release $\text{OH}^-$; some compounds like ammonia produce $\text{OH}^-$ by reacting with water.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Base dissociation: $\text{BOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{B}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq)$. Strong bases dissociate completely; weak bases establish equilibrium.

examples.explanation: Strong base: $\text{NaOH}(s) \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{Na}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq)$. Dissociates 100%; high conductivity.

image.caption: Strong bases dissociate completely to release $\text{OH}^-$.

examples.explanation: Weak base: $\text{NH}_3(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_4^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq)$. Produces $\text{OH}^-$ by accepting $\text{H}^+$ from water.

image.caption: Ammonia is a weak base that produces $\text{OH}^-$ indirectly.

examples.explanation: Strong base, low solubility: $\text{Ca(OH)}_2(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{Ca}^{2+}(aq) + 2\text{OH}^-(aq)$. What dissolves dissociates completely.

image.caption: Some strong bases have limited solubility.

categories.type: Strong vs. Weak Acids and Bases
categories.description: Strength refers to the degree of ionization/dissociation in water, not concentration. Strong acids/bases ionize completely; weak acids/bases ionize partially.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Strong: equilibrium lies far to products ($K \gg 1$); weak: equilibrium favors reactants ($K \ll 1$). Strength is an intrinsic property; concentration is adjustable.

examples.explanation: $\text{HCl}$, $\text{HBr}$, $\text{HI}$, $\text{HNO}_3$, $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ (first $\text{H}^+$), $\text{HClO}_4$. Memorize these six common strong acids.

image.caption: Only six common acids are strong; all others are weak.

examples.explanation: Group 1 hydroxides ($\text{LiOH}$, $\text{NaOH}$, $\text{KOH}$, etc.) and heavy Group 2 hydroxides ($\text{Ca(OH)}_2$, $\text{Sr(OH)}_2$, $\text{Ba(OH)}_2$).

image.caption: Strong bases are metal hydroxides of Groups 1 and 2.

principles.statement: Free $\text{H}^+$ ions do not exist in water; they immediately bind to water molecules to form hydronium ions ($\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$). Chemists often write $\text{H}^+(aq)$ as shorthand for $\text{H}_3\text{O}^+(aq)$.
principles.application: Acid ionization is more accurately written: $\text{HCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_3\text{O}^+ + \text{Cl}^-$, not $\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{H}^+ + \text{Cl}^-$. Both notations are used; understand the underlying reality.
principles.analogy: Like a celebrity with bodyguards: $\text{H}^+$ is never alone in water; it's always surrounded by water molecules as $\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$.

principles.statement: Acid/base strength (strong/weak) refers to degree of ionization; concentration (concentrated/dilute) refers to amount of solute per volume of solution. These are independent properties.
principles.application: A dilute solution of strong acid (0.001 M $\text{HCl}$) is still a strong acid (100% ionized) but has low $[\text{H}^+]$. A concentrated solution of weak acid (10 M $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$) is still weak (partially ionized) but has higher total acid content.
principles.example: This distinction is crucial for understanding pH, reactivity, and safety: concentrated weak acids can be more hazardous than dilute strong acids.

sections.section_title: Common Acids and Bases in Everyday Life
sections.introduction: Acids and bases are not just laboratory chemicals—they are essential to food, cleaning products, medicine, and biological processes. Recognizing common examples connects classroom chemistry to daily experience.

key_concept.definition: Many household and biological substances are acids or bases: citric acid in fruits, acetic acid in vinegar, carbonic acid in soda, lactic acid in muscles, hydrochloric acid in stomach; sodium hydroxide in drain cleaners, ammonia in cleaners, sodium bicarbonate in baking, magnesium hydroxide in antacids.
key_concept.context: Understanding these common examples helps appreciate the relevance of acid-base chemistry and reinforces safety awareness.

categories.description: Acids found in food, beverages, biological systems, and household products.
categories.detailed_mechanism: These acids provide flavor, preservation, or biological function; most are weak acids safe for consumption in normal amounts.

examples.explanation: Found in citrus fruits; weak triprotic acid; provides sour taste and acts as preservative; formula: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_8\text{O}_7 \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{C}_6\text{H}_7\text{O}_7^-$.

image.caption: Citric acid gives lemons and oranges their sour taste.

examples.explanation: Active ingredient in vinegar (5% solution); weak acid; used in cooking, cleaning, and food preservation.

image.caption: Acetic acid is the sour component of vinegar.

examples.explanation: Forms when $\text{CO}_2$ dissolves in water: $\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$. Gives carbonated beverages their tang; unstable, decomposes to $\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$.

image.caption: Carbonic acid provides the fizz and tang in sodas.

examples.explanation: Produced in muscles during intense exercise; weak acid; causes muscle fatigue; also in yogurt and fermented foods.

image.caption: Lactic acid buildup causes muscle soreness.

examples.explanation: Strong acid; gastric juice contains ~0.1 M $\text{HCl}$; aids digestion, kills pathogens; protected by stomach lining.

image.caption: Stomach acid aids digestion and protects against pathogens.

categories.description: Bases found in cleaning products, medicine, food, and biological systems.
categories.detailed_mechanism: These bases provide cleaning action, neutralize acids, or serve biological functions; strength varies from mild to caustic.

examples.explanation: Baking soda; weak base; reacts with acids to produce $\text{CO}_2$ (leavening in baking); neutralizes stomach acid (antacid).

image.caption: Baking soda is a versatile weak base.

examples.explanation: Weak base; common in glass and household cleaners; pungent odor; effective at cutting grease.

image.caption: Ammonia is a common ingredient in household cleaners.

examples.explanation: Active ingredient in milk of magnesia; weak base; neutralizes excess stomach acid; low solubility makes it gentle.

image.caption: Magnesium hydroxide neutralizes stomach acid.

examples.explanation: Strong base; found in drain cleaners and oven cleaners; highly caustic; dissolves grease by saponification.

image.caption: Strong bases like NaOH are powerful but dangerous cleaners.

examples.explanation: Slaked lime; used in mortar, plaster, and water treatment; moderately strong base; adjusts pH in agriculture.

image.caption: Calcium hydroxide has construction and agricultural uses.

categories.description: Acids and bases play critical roles in biological systems, from digestion to cellular metabolism.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Biological systems maintain precise pH balance; acids and bases regulate enzyme activity, nutrient absorption, and waste elimination.

examples.explanation: Blood pH maintained at 7.35-7.45 by bicarbonate buffer: $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{HCO}_3^-$. Critical for oxygen transport and enzyme function.

image.caption: Blood pH is tightly regulated by buffer systems.

examples.explanation: Stomach $\text{HCl}$ (pH ~1.5-3.5) aids digestion; antacids ($\text{Mg(OH)}_2$, $\text{CaCO}_3$) neutralize excess acid to relieve heartburn.

image.caption: Antacids neutralize excess stomach acid.

principles.statement: Many foods contain acids or bases that affect flavor, texture, and preservation. Acids provide sour taste and inhibit bacterial growth; bases can affect texture and neutralize acids.
principles.application: Vinegar (acetic acid) preserves pickles; baking soda (base) helps cookies rise by reacting with acids to produce $\text{CO}_2$; citric acid enhances flavor and prevents browning.
principles.example: Understanding these roles helps in cooking, food science, and nutrition.

principles.principle: Safety with Household Acids and Bases
principles.statement: Even common household acids and bases can be hazardous. Strong bases (drain cleaners) are more dangerous than strong acids due to deeper tissue penetration; always follow label instructions.
principles.application: Never mix bleach (base) with acids (vinegar, toilet cleaner)—produces toxic chlorine gas. Wear gloves and eye protection when using strong cleaners.
principles.analogy: Like tools: a knife (acid) can cut, but a chainsaw (strong base) requires extra caution—both are useful but demand respect.

sections.introduction: Apply acid-base concepts to identify compounds, predict properties, and explain everyday observations.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Acids and Bases from Formulas
worked_examples.problem: Classify each compound as an Arrhenius acid, Arrhenius base, or neither: (a) $\text{HNO}_3$, (b) $\text{KOH}$, (c) $\text{CH}_4$, (d) $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$, (e) $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$.

step_by_step.explanation: Contains H at beginning of formula; ionizes to $\text{H}^+ + \text{NO}_3^-$. Strong acid.
Metal hydroxide; dissociates to $\text{K}^+ + \text{OH}^-$. Strong base.
Hydrocarbon; no ionizable H or $\text{OH}$ group; does not produce $\text{H}^+$ or $\text{OH}^-$ in water.
Metal hydroxide; dissociates to $\text{Ca}^{2+} + 2\text{OH}^-$. Strong base.
Glucose; contains H but not ionizable (bonded to C, not as $\text{-COOH}$); does not produce $\text{H}^+$ or $\text{OH}^-$.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) $\text{HNO}_3$: Arrhenius acid; (b) $\text{KOH}$: Arrhenius base; (c) $\text{CH}_4$: neither; (d) $\text{Ca(OH)}_2$: Arrhenius base; (e) $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$: neither. Arrhenius acids have ionizable H (usually written first); Arrhenius bases contain $\text{OH}^-$ or produce it in water.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Litmus Test Results
worked_examples.problem: Predict the color change (if any) when each solution is tested with blue and red litmus paper: (a) $\text{HCl}(aq)$, (b) $\text{NaOH}(aq)$, (c) $\text{NaCl}(aq)$, (d) $\text{NH}_3(aq)$.

step_by_step.calculation: Blue litmus → red; red litmus → no change
Blue litmus → no change; red litmus → blue

step_by_step.explanation: Salt of strong acid + strong base; neutral solution; no $\text{H}^+$ or $\text{OH}^-$ produced.
Weak base; produces $\text{OH}^-$ via $\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_4^+ + \text{OH}^-$; basic solution.
step_by_step.calculation: Blue litmus → no change; red litmus → blue

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) $\text{HCl}$: blue → red, red → no change (acid); (b) $\text{NaOH}$: blue → no change, red → blue (base); (c) $\text{NaCl}$: no change for either (neutral); (d) $\text{NH}_3$: blue → no change, red → blue (weak base). Litmus tests distinguish acids (red), bases (blue), and neutral solutions (no change).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Litmus Test Prediction Based on Acid-Base Classification

worked_examples.title: Writing Acid Ionization Equations
worked_examples.problem: Write the ionization equation for each acid in water: (a) nitric acid ($\text{HNO}_3$), (b) acetic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$).

step_by_step.explanation: Strong acid; ionizes completely. Use single arrow. Include water as reactant to show hydronium formation.
Weak acid; ionizes partially. Use equilibrium arrow. Only the carboxyl H is ionizable.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) $\text{HNO}_3(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow \text{H}_3\text{O}^+(aq) + \text{NO}_3^-(aq)$ (strong acid, complete ionization); (b) $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_3\text{O}^+(aq) + \text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-(aq)$ (weak acid, equilibrium). The arrow type indicates strength: single arrow for strong, equilibrium arrow for weak.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing Acid Ionization Equations with Proper Arrow Notation

worked_examples.title: Explaining Everyday Acid-Base Phenomena
worked_examples.problem: Explain each observation using acid-base concepts: (a) Lemon juice cleans tarnished copper; (b) Baking soda neutralizes spilled vinegar; (c) Antacids relieve heartburn.

step_by_step.explanation: Lemon juice contains citric acid; tarnish on copper is often copper oxide ($\text{CuO}$). Acid reacts with metal oxide: $\text{CuO} + 2\text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$, dissolving tarnish.

Baking soda ($\text{NaHCO}_3$, base) reacts with vinegar (acetic acid): $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{NaHCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COONa} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2$. Neutralization produces salt, water, and $\text{CO}_2$ gas (fizzing).

Heartburn is excess stomach $\text{HCl}$. Antacids contain bases like $\text{Mg(OH)}_2$: $\text{Mg(OH)}_2 + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Neutralization reduces acidity, relieving discomfort.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Citric acid in lemon juice reacts with copper oxide tarnish, dissolving it as soluble copper salt. (b) Baking soda (base) neutralizes acetic acid (vinegar) in a neutralization reaction that produces salt, water, and $\text{CO}_2$ gas. (c) Antacids contain bases that neutralize excess stomach $\text{HCl}$, reducing acidity and relieving heartburn. All three examples illustrate acid-base neutralization reactions.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying Acid-Base Concepts to Real-World Phenomena

worked_examples.title: Distinguishing Strong and Weak Acids
worked_examples.problem: Two solutions have the same concentration: 0.1 M $\text{HCl}$ and 0.1 M $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$. Which has lower pH? Which conducts electricity better? Explain.

step_by_step.explanation: $\text{HCl}$ is strong acid: 100% ionized → $[\text{H}^+] = 0.1~\text{M}$. $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ is weak acid: ~1% ionized → $[\text{H}^+] \approx 0.001~\text{M}$.
pH = $-\log[\text{H}^+]$. Higher $[\text{H}^+]$ gives lower pH. $\text{HCl}$ has higher $[\text{H}^+]$, so lower pH.

Conductivity depends on ion concentration. $\text{HCl}$ produces more ions ($\text{H}^+$, $\text{Cl}^-$) than $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ ($\text{H}^+$, $\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-$, plus mostly unionized molecules).

worked_examples.final_answer: 0.1 M $\text{HCl}$ has lower pH (~1.0) and conducts electricity better than 0.1 M $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ (pH ~3.0). Although concentrations are equal, $\text{HCl}$ is a strong acid (complete ionization) producing more $\text{H}^+$ ions, while $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ is weak (partial ionization) producing fewer ions. Strength, not concentration, determines $[\text{H}^+]$ and conductivity.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Strong vs. Weak Acid: Ionization, pH, and Conductivity

sections.introduction: Avoid these frequent errors when learning about acids and bases; apply expert strategies for accurate identification and prediction.

key_concept.definition: Common acid-base errors include: confusing strength with concentration, misidentifying acids by formula alone, forgetting that not all H-containing compounds are acids, and overlooking safety with household chemicals.
key_concept.context: Careful attention to definitions, ionization behavior, and safety protocols prevents conceptual and practical errors.

characteristics: Assuming concentrated acid is always strong
Thinking dilute strong acid is 'weak'
Mixing up intrinsic property (strength) with adjustable property (concentration)
Misinterpreting pH or reactivity data
classifications.behavior: Strength is about % ionization; concentration is about moles per liter. A dilute strong acid can have higher $[\text{H}^+]$ than a concentrated weak acid, but the strong acid is still 100% ionized.

examples.explanation: Wrong: '10 M acetic acid is stronger.' Correct: $\text{HCl}$ is strong (100% ionized) even when dilute; $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ is weak (~1% ionized) even when concentrated.

image.caption: Strength is intrinsic; concentration is adjustable.

classifications.type: Mistake: Assuming All H-Containing Compounds Are Acids
classifications.value: Only Ionizable H Makes an Acid
characteristics: Calling $\text{CH}_4$ an acid because it has H
Assuming $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$ is acidic
Not recognizing that H must be ionizable (bonded to electronegative atom)
Misclassifying organic compounds
classifications.behavior: Only H atoms bonded to electronegative atoms (O, Cl, etc.) in specific arrangements (e.g., $\text{-COOH}$) are ionizable as acids. H bonded to C (as in hydrocarbons) is not acidic.

examples.explanation: Wrong: 'Both have H, so both are acids.' Correct: $\text{CH}_4$ H is bonded to C (not ionizable); $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$ has ionizable H in $\text{-COOH}$ group.

image.caption: Only specific H atoms are acidic.

characteristics: Assuming only compounds with $\text{OH}$ in formula are bases
Missing that $\text{NH}_3$ is a base despite no $\text{OH}$ in formula
Not recognizing proton-acceptor behavior
Limiting base identification to Arrhenius definition only
classifications.behavior: Arrhenius bases produce $\text{OH}^-$, but some (like $\text{NH}_3$) do so by reacting with water. Later definitions (Brønsted-Lowry) expand base concept to any proton acceptor.

examples.explanation: Wrong: '$\text{NH}_3$ has no $\text{OH}$, so not a base.' Correct: $\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{NH}_4^+ + \text{OH}^-$; it produces $\text{OH}^-$, so it's an Arrhenius base.

image.caption: Ammonia is a base that produces $\text{OH}^-$ indirectly.

classifications.type: Mistake: Ignoring Safety with Household Chemicals
characteristics: Assuming household acids/bases are harmless
Mixing cleaners without checking compatibility
Not wearing PPE with strong bases
Tasting or smelling chemicals directly
classifications.behavior: Even common chemicals can be hazardous: drain cleaners (strong bases) cause severe burns; mixing bleach with acids produces toxic gas. Always follow safety guidelines.

examples.explanation: Wrong: 'Both are cleaners, so mixing is fine.' Correct: Bleach ($\text{NaOCl}$, base) + vinegar (acetic acid) → toxic $\text{Cl}_2$ gas. Never mix cleaners unless label says it's safe.

image.caption: Never mix household cleaners without verifying compatibility.

classifications.type: Pro Tip: Use the Acid-Base Identification Checklist
characteristics: Formula starts with H? → Likely acid (check if ionizable)
Formula ends with OH and has metal? → Likely base
Produces $\text{H}^+$ in water? → Acid
Produces $\text{OH}^-$ or accepts $\text{H}^+$? → Base
Litmus test: red = acid, blue = base, no change = neutral
classifications.behavior: This checklist combines formula analysis, ionization behavior, and experimental tests for reliable identification.

examples.explanation: Formula: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ → starts with H, known strong acid → acid. Litmus: blue → red → confirms acid. Conductivity: high → strong electrolyte → strong acid.

image.caption: Systematic checklist ensures accurate classification.

classifications.type: Pro Tip: Memorize the Six Strong Acids
characteristics: $\text{HCl}$, $\text{HBr}$, $\text{HI}$ (hydrohalic acids, except HF)
$\text{HNO}_3$ (nitric acid)
$\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ (sulfuric acid, first $\text{H}^+$ only)
$\text{HClO}_4$ (perchloric acid)
All other acids are weak
Strong acids ionize 100%; weak acids establish equilibrium
classifications.behavior: Knowing the strong acids allows quick prediction of ionization behavior, pH, and conductivity without memorizing every compound.

examples.explanation: Not on the six-strong-acids list → weak acid. Partial ionization: $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4 \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{PO}_4^-$. pH higher than strong acid of same concentration.

image.caption: Memorizing these six simplifies acid classification.

principles.statement: Acid properties (sour, litmus red, conductive, metal-reactive) all stem from $\text{H}^+$ ions; base properties (bitter, slippery, litmus blue, conductive) all stem from $\text{OH}^-$ ions or proton acceptance.
principles.application: If a solution conducts electricity and turns litmus red, it contains $\text{H}^+$ → it's an acid. If it conducts and turns litmus blue, it contains $\text{OH}^-$ or accepts $\text{H}^+$ → it's a base.
principles.analogy: Like fingerprints: multiple clues (properties) all point to the same identity (acid or base).

principles.principle: Arrhenius Definition Is a Starting Point
principles.statement: The Arrhenius definition (acids produce $\text{H}^+$, bases produce $\text{OH}^-$) is limited to aqueous solutions. Later definitions (Brønsted-Lowry, Lewis) expand the concept to broader contexts.
principles.application: In water, $\text{NH}_3$ is a base because it produces $\text{OH}^-$; in non-aqueous solvents, the Brønsted-Lowry definition (proton acceptor) is more useful.
principles.example: Understanding the limitations of definitions helps transition to more advanced acid-base theories.

summary: Acids and bases are fundamental classes of compounds with opposing properties. Arrhenius acids produce $\text{H}^+$ ions in water; Arrhenius bases produce $\text{OH}^-$ ions or accept $\text{H}^+$. Acids taste sour, turn litmus red, conduct electricity, and react with metals; bases taste bitter, feel slippery, turn litmus blue, and conduct electricity. Strong acids/bases ionize/dissociate completely; weak acids/bases establish equilibrium with partial ionization. Common examples include citric acid (fruits), acetic acid (vinegar), $\text{HCl}$ (stomach), $\text{NaOH}$ (cleaners), $\text{NH}_3$ (cleaners), and $\text{NaHCO}_3$ (baking). Critical skills include: identifying acids/bases from formulas and properties, writing ionization equations with proper arrow notation, distinguishing strength from concentration, and applying safety protocols with household chemicals. Common mistakes include confusing strength with concentration, assuming all H-containing compounds are acids, and overlooking that bases include compounds like $\text{NH}_3$ that produce $\text{OH}^-$ indirectly. Mastery of acid-base fundamentals enables understanding of pH, neutralization, buffers, and advanced acid-base theories.
